{{Short description|Quantum field theory of the electromagnetic interaction}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Quantum electrodynamics
| image = Feynman diagram - Moller scattering 01.svg
| caption = [[Feynman diagram]] for electron–electron scattering via photon exchange.
| topics = [[Quantum mechanics]], [[Electromagnetism]], [[Photon]], [[Special relativity]]
| key_equation = [[Dirac equation]], [[Quantization (physics)|quantized]] [[electromagnetic field]]
}}

'''Quantum electrodynamics''' ('''QED''') is the [[relativistic]] [[quantum field theory]] of the [[electromagnetic interaction]]. It describes how [[light]] and [[matter]] interact and is the first theory to achieve full agreement between [[quantum mechanics]] and [[special relativity]]. QED mathematically describes all phenomena involving electrically charged particles interacting by means of [[photon]] exchange.

The theory was developed in the late 1940s by [[Richard Feynman]], [[Julian Schwinger]], [[Shin'ichirō Tomonaga]], and [[Freeman Dyson]], who received the [[Nobel Prize in Physics]] in 1965 for their work.

== Fundamental interaction ==
In QED, the electromagnetic force between two charged particles (such as [[electron]]s) is mediated by the exchange of virtual [[photon]]s. The fundamental vertex describes an electron emitting or absorbing a photon:

:<math>e^- \leftrightarrow e^- + \gamma</math>

The strength of the interaction is governed by the [[fine-structure constant]]:
:<math>\alpha = \frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \hbar c} \approx \frac{1}{137}</math>

== Lagrangian ==
The QED [[Lagrangian (field theory)|Lagrangian]] density for a spin-1/2 field <math>\psi</math> (electron) interacting with the electromagnetic field <math>A_\mu</math> is:
:<math>\mathcal{L} = \bar{\psi}(i\gamma^\mu\partial_\mu - m)\psi - \frac{1}{4}F_{\mu\nu}F^{\mu\nu} - e\bar{\psi}\gamma^\mu\psi A_\mu</math>

== Feynman diagrams ==
{{Main|Feynman diagram}}
[[Richard Feynman]] introduced a pictorial method for calculating scattering amplitudes. Key QED processes include:
* Electron–electron (Møller) scattering
* Electron–positron (Bhabha) scattering
* [[Compton scattering]] (<math>e^- + \gamma \rightarrow e^- + \gamma</math>)
* [[Pair production]] (<math>\gamma \rightarrow e^- + e^+</math>)
* [[Bremsstrahlung]] (braking radiation)

== Renormalization ==
QED achieves finite predictions through [[renormalization]] — absorbing infinite quantities into physical constants (mass, charge). This was a major theoretical breakthrough that made QED a predictive theory.

== Precision tests ==
QED is the most precisely tested theory in physics:
* [[Anomalous magnetic dipole moment]] of the electron: theory matches experiment to 1 part in 10¹²
* [[Lamb shift]] in the hydrogen spectrum
* Hyperfine splitting in [[positronium]]

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Electromagnetism]]
* [[Photon]]
* [[Quantum chromodynamics]]
* [[Quantum field theory]]
* [[Standard model]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Quantum electrodynamics| ]]
[[Category:Quantum field theory]]
[[Category:Electromagnetism]]
[[Category:Concepts in physics]]
